1: 'On that day there shall be a fountain opened for the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, to cleanse them from sin and uncleanness.' The fountain for sin (maqor niftach) flows directly from the mourning of Zechariah 12:10-14. The recognition and mourning over the pierced one is immediately followed by the opening of a fountain (maqor, H4726 — a spring, a source) for cleansing. This is the NT's cleansing through the blood of Christ: 'the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin' (1 John 1:7). The fountain's dual purpose: for chata't (sin, H2403) and for niddah (impurity/uncleanness, H5079 — the Levitical term for ritual uncleanness, especially menstrual impurity). The atonement covers both moral guilt and ritual uncleanness.
2: 'And on that day, declares the LORD of hosts, I will cut off the names of the idols from the land, so that they shall be remembered no more. And also I will remove from the land the prophets and the unclean spirit.' The purification of the land follows the opening of the cleansing fountain: the idol-names (shemot ha'atzabbim) cut off (= no longer remembered, no longer worshiped), the false prophets removed, and the unclean spirit (ruach hatumah) expelled. The sequence — fountain for sin (v1), removal of idols and false prophecy (v2-6) — is the personal and social purification that accompanies the eschatological atonement.
3: 'And if anyone again prophesies, his father and mother who bore him will say to him, You shall not live, for you speak lies in the name of the LORD. And his father and mother who bore him shall pierce him through when he prophesies.' The severity of false prophecy in the restored community: even parents will execute a false-prophet son (cf. Deuteronomy 13:6-11: even a beloved family member who leads to idolatry must be stoned). The post-restoration community has such a heightened awareness of the cost of false prophecy (the exile was partly caused by false prophets, Jeremiah 14:13-16; Ezek 13) that the most intimate relationships cannot shield a false prophet from judgment.
4: 'On that day every prophet will be ashamed of his vision when he prophesies. He will not put on a hairy cloak in order to deceive.' The false prophet's self-exposure: no longer wearing the prophetic uniform (aderet se'ar, a hairy cloak — Elijah's distinctive dress, 2 Kings 1:8; cf. Matthew 3:4, John the Baptist's camel-hair clothing as prophetic identification). The false prophet will be ashamed to claim prophetic status and will abandon the distinguishing garment of the prophetic office.
5: 'But he will say, I am no prophet, I am a worker of the soil, for a man sold me as a slave in my youth.' The false prophet's self-denial: claiming to be a farmer (oved adamah ani), not a prophet. The reference to being sold as a slave in youth may reflect the practice of debt-slavery — the claim is an attempt to disavow prophetic credentials entirely. The irony: the false prophet who sold his prophetic services to the highest bidder now claims he was the one who was sold.
6: 'And if someone asks him, What are these wounds on your back? he will say, The wounds I received in the house of my friends.' The wounds (makkot) on the back/hands of the false prophet may be the marks of ecstatic prophetic self-wounding (1 Kings 18:28: the Baal prophets cut themselves) or the marks of discipline received from those who recognized his false prophecy. 'The house of my friends' (beit ahavai) is the false prophet's evasive answer: the wounds came from friends, not from punishment for false prophecy. The verse may also be read as a standalone Christological passage: the wounds received in the house of friends echo John 20:25 (the marks of the nails in Jesus's hands) and the betrayal context (John 13:18: even my close friend... has lifted his heel against me).
7: 'Awake, O sword, against my shepherd, against the man who stands next to me, declares the LORD of hosts. Strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered; I will turn my hand against the little ones.' The sword oracle against the shepherd is the second most-cited verse from Zechariah in the passion narratives (after Zechariah 12:10). Jesus quotes it directly in Gethsemane: 'Strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered' (Matthew 26:31; Mark 14:27). Key terms: (1) herev uri (O sword, awake!) — the sword as YHWH's instrument of judgment; (2) al ro'i (against my shepherd) — YHWH's own shepherd is struck; (3) al ha'ish amiti (against the man who stands next to me/my associate) — amiti (H5997) = my fellow, my associate, my close companion. This is one who stands beside YHWH in intimate relationship — not a distant servant but a divine associate. The NT identification: Christ as both YHWH's shepherd and his divine associate (amiti), struck by the sword of YHWH's judgment as the bearer of the world's sin (Isaiah 53:4-6).
8: 'In the whole land, declares the LORD, two thirds shall be cut off and perish, and one third shall be left alive.' The two-thirds/one-third division: YHWH's refining judgment reduces the population to a remnant — two-thirds cut off and dying, one-third surviving. This is more severe than Noah's flood (in which 8 survived) but produces a refined remnant, not a purged community. The one-third who survive are the remnant who go through the refining fire. The two-thirds are not the 'more wicked'; the distinction is eschatological, not strictly moral — it is the remnant theology that runs throughout the prophets (Isaiah 1:9; Zechariah 6:13; Zechariah 10:20-21; Amos 5:3).
9: 'And I will put this third into the fire, and refine them as one refines silver, and test them as gold is tested. They will call upon my name, and I will answer them. I will say, They are my people; and they will say, The LORD is my God.' The remnant is refined in fire — not destroyed but purified. The silver-refining and gold-testing metaphors (Psalms 66:10; Isaiah 48:10; Malachi 3:2-3) describe the process of purification through suffering: impurities burned away, the precious metal revealed. The chapter climaxes with the covenant formula restored: hemmah yiqre'u vishmi va'ani e'eneh otam amarti ammi hemma vehem yomru YHWH Elohai (they shall call on my name and I will answer; I will say, they are my people; they will say, YHWH is my God). The broken covenant — shattered in Zechariah 7-11 — is restored in its simplest, most intimate form: the mutual declaration of belonging. This is the covenant formula of Hosea 2:23 ('I will say, You are my people; and he shall say, You are my God') completed through the fire of the cross.
